title: The Frontier Beylik
bullets: Osman I, the ancestor of every Ottoman sultan who followed, was the chieftain of a small Turkic warrior band in northwestern Anatolia whose actual life can barely be reconstructed from contemporary sources — even the date of his death is uncertain.

The political order that made the Ottoman founding possible was the Mongol Ilkhanate's crushing of the Seljuk Sultanate of Rum at the Battle of Köse Dağ in 1243, leaving a patchwork of roughly two dozen small Turkic beyliks across Anatolia.

Osman's first notable military victory came at the Battle of Bapheus in 1302, the first time his beylik had defeated a regular Byzantine army in the open field, which shocked Constantinople enough to provoke the disastrous hire of the Catalan Company.

The gaza ideology — organized armed raiding against Christians on behalf of Islam, conducted by frontier warriors called ghazis — was the Ottoman beylik's core recruiting pitch, drawing volunteers from across Anatolia and even Christian fighters who had fallen out with their own lords.

The early Ottoman armies were deliberately multi-ethnic and multi-confessional, containing Greek, Slavic, and Albanian Christian warriors alongside Turkic and heterodox Muslim recruits, because loyalty and fighting ability mattered more than religious purity in the founding period.

Osman's Sufi connections through his father-in-law Sheikh Edebali of the heterodox Babai network gave the early Ottoman state religious legitimacy without the doctrinal narrowness of established urban Sunni Islam.

Orhan, who inherited the beylik on Osman's death in 1326, transformed it from a raiding band into an organized polity by taking Bursa as the first proper capital, minting Ottoman silver akces, and commissioning the first identifiably Ottoman architecture.

Orhan's acquisition of Nicaea in 1331 — the city of the First Ecumenical Council and the Byzantine government-in-exile capital after 1204 — was a major symbolic blow to Constantinople that it could not absorb.

The turning point from Anatolian beylik to European power came in March 1354 when Orhan's son Süleyman Pasha occupied the earthquake-ruined Byzantine fortress at Gallipoli and refused to leave, giving the Ottomans their first permanent European foothold.

title: Conquering the Balkans
bullets: Murad I, who ruled from 1362 to 1389, was the first Ottoman sultan whose actions can be tracked in detail across multiple source traditions, and virtually every institutional feature that would define the empire for the next four centuries was either invented or formalized on his watch.
Taking Adrianople in 1369 and making it the empire's working capital was a decisive choice: the Ottoman state was now a European empire with Anatolian roots, and it would remain so until 1453.

The Second Bulgarian Empire, weakened by a century of Hungarian invasions and fragmented into three competing kingdoms, was picked off one at a time through the 1370s and 1380s until effective Bulgarian independence ceased to exist.

The Battle of Kosovo on June 15, 1389, killed both Murad I and Serbian Prince Lazar Hrebeljanović, destroyed any prospect of Serbian-led Balkan resistance, and launched one of the most consequential national myths in modern European political history.

Bayezid I, nicknamed Yıldırım (the Thunderbolt) for the speed of his armies, eliminated rival Anatolian beyliks, blockaded Constantinople from 1394, and built the Anadoluhisarı fortress on the Bosporus — moves that doubled the empire's territory but offended the Turkic religious establishment.

The Battle of Nicopolis on September 25, 1396, was the worst Christian defeat against the Ottomans since Manzikert — Sigismund of Hungary escaped down the Danube, and Bayezid's theatrical execution of thousands of prisoners ended any prospect of large-scale European intervention for a generation.

The Janissary Corps, created under Murad I around 1363, was the first Ottoman standing professional infantry — recruited from converted Christian boys, trained for coordinated firearms use, and paid from the central treasury rather than from land grants.

The devshirme system, formalized under Murad I as a regular tribute levied on the empire's Balkan Christian subjects, collected boys aged eight to eighteen for conversion to Islam, training in the imperial palace, and careers in the military and administrative elite.

title: The Tamerlane Catastrophe
bullets: Timur, ruling from Samarkand and commanding the largest single state in Eurasia outside the Ming dynasty, was drawn to Anatolia by a combination of displaced bey-refugees seeking restoration and a series of increasingly hostile letters with Bayezid that had the quality of two confident conquerors stress-testing each other — and Bayezid fatally misjudged the test.

At the Battle of Ankara on July 28, 1402, the Ottoman army was destroyed by a combination of midsummer thirst, mass defection of Anatolian cavalry who recognized their deposed beys riding in Timur's ranks, and war elephants brought from Timur's Indian campaigns.

Bayezid I, the unstoppable conqueror of the Christian Balkans, was captured alive at Ankara and died in Timur's custody on March 8, 1403, approximately eight months after his capture — the contemporary details of his treatment (the golden cage, the footstool) are likely European literary embellishment rather than documented fact.

Timur had no interest in administering the territory he had taken; he restored several beyliks Bayezid had absorbed, installed preferred candidates among Bayezid's sons, and withdrew east, dying in 1405 while preparing an invasion of Ming China.

The eleven-year Interregnum (1402–1413) saw Bayezid's surviving sons fight a four-cornered civil war; the eventual winner, Mehmed Çelebi, won by being the most patient, securing the loyalty of the Janissary corps, and strangling his last rival Musa with a bowstring after a decisive battle in Bulgaria.

Mehmed I spent eight years cleaning up the wreckage — restoring provincial authority, putting down the syncretic Sheikh Bedreddin peasant uprising of 1416, and rebuilding the army and treasury — and was later called the second founder of the dynasty.

Murad II, who took the throne in 1421, conducted the first real Ottoman gunpowder artillery bombardment of the Theodosian Walls in 1422, demonstrating the principle that masonry could eventually be broken by cannon fire even though the technology was still too primitive to succeed.

The Battle of Varna on November 10, 1444, destroyed the last serious medieval crusader army, killed the Polish-Hungarian king Władysław III (whose head was put on a spear and paraded around the field), and rendered Constantinople's diplomatic isolation complete.

title: The Conquest of Constantinople
bullets: Mehmed II came to the throne at nineteen, took Constantinople at twenty-one, and spent the next twenty-eight years remaking the Ottoman state into a world empire, claiming the title Kayser-i Rûm (Caesar of Rome) as the legitimate successor of the Roman emperors.

Rumelihisarı, the European-side fortress sealing the Bosporus, was constructed in just over four months in the spring and summer of 1452, combined with the existing Anadoluhisarı to close the strait, and cut off Constantinople's Black Sea grain supply.

The Hungarian engineer Orban, who had first offered his services to Constantine XI and been told the Byzantine treasury could not pay his price, cast the largest cannon the world had yet seen for Mehmed — a barrel roughly twenty-seven feet long throwing a stone ball of about twelve hundred pounds, requiring sixty oxen to move.

The Ottoman dragging of seventy ships overland on a greased wooden ramp on the night of April 22, bypassing the Golden Horn chain, created a new front that forced the defenders to spread their already-thin manpower across additional wall sections.

Constantinople fell on May 29, 1453, when the Genoese commander Giustiniani was wounded and his troops began withdrawing; Constantine XI was last seen rushing into the press of Ottoman troops at the breach, and his body was never definitely identified.

Mehmed cut the authorized three days of plunder to one, rode into Hagia Sophia on the afternoon of May 29, ordered it consecrated as a mosque, and then reportedly quoted a Persian couplet about the impermanence of imperial glory — one of the more melancholic moments of triumph in conqueror's history.

Topkapı Palace, begun in 1459, became the working residence of Ottoman sultans for nearly four hundred years — not a single grand structure on the European model but a complex of pavilions, courtyards, and gardens organized around three successive courts of increasing privacy.

The Kanunname of 1477 codified the fratricide law — empowering new sultans to legally execute their brothers and nephews — and organized the timar system of revocable land grants to sipahi cavalry in exchange for hereditary military service.

Mehmed's later campaigns took the Empire of Trebizond in 1461, fought Venice for sixteen years across the Aegean, and in 1480 landed an invasion force that held a beachhead on the Italian mainland at Otranto for thirteen months before his death ended the campaign.

title: Selim, Süleyman, the Lawgiver
bullets: Bayezid II opened the Ottoman Empire to the roughly 200,000 Sephardic Jews expelled from Spain by Ferdinand and Isabella's Alhambra Decree of 1492, reportedly sending ships to Iberian ports and remarking that Ferdinand had impoverished his own country and enriched Bayezid's.

Salonica by 1500 had become the largest Sephardic Jewish city in the world, with the Jewish population outnumbering Greeks and Turks combined, and Sephardic printers established the first Hebrew presses in the Ottoman Empire in the 1490s — decades before Arabic-script printing was permitted.

Selim I, called the Grim, doubled the empire's size in eight years of nearly continuous campaigning, taking the throne from his father under duress in 1512 and dying of an infected boil in 1520 at age forty-nine.

The Battle of Chaldiran on August 23, 1514, decided by Ottoman artillery against the Safavid cavalry force of Shah Ismail I, institutionalized the Sunni-Shi'a divide as the operative religious geopolitics of the Islamic world — a divide still operating in modern Middle Eastern politics.

At Marj Dabiq in 1516 and Ridaniya in 1517, Ottoman gunpowder superiority destroyed the Mamluk Sultanate of Egypt, which had dominated the eastern Mediterranean for three centuries but had not adopted firearms; the last Mamluk sultan Tuman Bay II was hanged from the Bab Zuwayla gate of his own capital.

The acquisition of Egypt gave Selim the Hijaz with Mecca and Medina, the title Servant of the Two Sanctuaries, and the Caliphate transferred from the last shadow Abbasid — fusing the religious and political legitimacy of Islam in a single Ottoman ruler for the first time.

Süleyman the Magnificent, who ruled for forty-six years (the longest reign in Ottoman history) and led thirteen major campaigns in person, took Belgrade in 1521, Rhodes in 1522, destroyed the Kingdom of Hungary at Mohács in 1526 killing King Louis II, and reached the walls of Vienna in 1529 before his supply lines gave out.

Ebussuud Efendi, Süleyman's Sheikh ul-Islam, spent decades harmonizing sharia with the kanun secular imperial law code to produce the Kanun-i Osmani — a unified legal framework applied by Ottoman judges from Cairo to Sarajevo to Buda.

Mimar Sinan, a devshirme recruit of probable Armenian or Greek origin from Cappadocia, served as chief imperial architect for forty-nine years and designed the Süleymaniye Mosque (1557) and the Selimiye Mosque in Edirne (1574), whose 108-foot dome surpassed Hagia Sophia's and which Sinan called the work of his maturity.

title: The Janissaries, the Devshirme, the Millet
bullets: The Janissary corps, from its founding around 1363 to its destruction in 1826, was for most of those four hundred and sixty-three years the most consistently effective professional infantry in the world — the first large force trained from childhood to use firearms in coordinated volley formations.

The devshirme collected boys aged eight to eighteen from the empire's Balkan Christian subjects approximately every four to seven years, with specific criteria excluding only sons, married youths, urban families with administrative connections, and non-Orthodox minorities — targeting the Orthodox Christian peasantry almost exclusively.

The most able devshirme boys entered the Enderun, the Inner Palace School at Topkapı, whose curriculum included seven academic-administrative chambers covering Ottoman Turkish, Arabic, Persian, mathematics, calligraphy, music, theology, and court etiquette over fourteen to twenty years of training.

Of approximately 215 grand viziers in Ottoman history, more than half were of devshirme origin, including Sokollu Mehmed Pasha (a Bosnian Serbian boy recruited around 1518) and the architect Mimar Sinan — the empire's administrative elite was a slave class recruited from its conquered Balkan Christian peasantry.

There are well-documented cases of Balkan Christian families bribing Ottoman officials to include their sons in the devshirme rather than exclude them — and well-documented cases of families hiding sons or marrying them off underage to avoid the levy — both responses existed simultaneously.

The millet system organized the empire's non-Muslim communities — Rum (Greek Orthodox), Armenian, Jewish, and Catholic — into recognized religious corporations with internal autonomy in personal status law, their own courts, and collective tax responsibility, managed by their own religious authorities.

The Ottoman bezant equivalent, the silver akce minted for three centuries, was the standard commercial denomination across an empire that was still roughly half non-Muslim as late as 1500 and forty percent non-Muslim in 1700 — the scale of religious diversity managed by this framework was unmatched in any contemporary state.

The framework that had been the empire's institutional strength for four hundred years — managing diversity without requiring homogeneity — became its critical weakness when nineteenth-century European nationalism transformed the Rum millet, the Armenian millet, and eventually the Muslim Turks themselves into national communities demanding political independence.

title: The Sultanate of Women and the Cage
bullets: The Sultanate of Women, running from approximately 1533 to 1683, was not a corruption of a previous imperial ideal but an adaptation to governing a fully formed multicontinental empire from a single capital, where the sultan's personal military leadership had become practically impossible.

Hürrem Sultan, born Roxelana in Ruthenia and captured as a teenage slave, was freed and legally married by Süleyman the Magnificent in an unprecedented public ceremony around 1533–1534, establishing the precedent of concentrated harem political power that her successors would institutionalize.

Kösem Sultan, born Anastasia to a Greek Orthodox priest's family on the island of Tinos, was the most consequential of the valide sultans — managing grand vizier appointments, controlling foreign policy negotiations, and directing major imperial decisions for most of the period from 1623 until her assassination by harem eunuchs in September 1651.

Mehmed III on his accession in 1595 executed nineteen of his brothers in a single ceremony, including toddlers, generating such popular outrage that the religious establishment — which had endorsed the fratricide law for over a century — expressed formal concern.

The Cage system, formalized under Ahmed I in 1603, confined the sultan's brothers and nephews to a specific Topkapı apartment complex under permanent house arrest with no military training, no administrative responsibility, and no contact with potential supporters.

Ibrahim I, brought out of the Kafes at age twenty-five after decades of confinement, was so traumatized that he initially refused to leave his room — convinced the messengers announcing his accession were assassins — and his subsequent eight-year reign was characterized by mental instability and erratic decision-making.

The Battle of Lepanto on October 7, 1571, destroyed perhaps two hundred Ottoman galleys and twenty-five thousand men, but Sokollu Mehmed Pasha rebuilt the fleet within a year and Cyprus remained Ottoman for the next three centuries — the battle's psychological impact on Christian Europe vastly exceeded its operational impact on Ottoman power.

The Blue Mosque of Ahmed I (1609–1616), built directly opposite Hagia Sophia on the site of the Byzantine Great Palace, with six minarets and over twenty thousand Iznik tiles, was the architectural climax of the late classical Ottoman period.

The Köprülü family, beginning with the eighty-one-year-old Mehmed Pasha Köprülü appointed grand vizier in 1656 on his own conditions of absolute authority, gave the empire a generation of decisive administration that halted the territorial drift of the mid-seventeenth century.

title: The Long Defeat
bullets: The Second Siege of Vienna, begun July 14, 1683, with about one hundred and twenty thousand men and three hundred cannon, was broken on September 12 by a Polish-German relief army of eighty-five thousand under King John III Sobieski, whose famous letter to the Pope read: Venimus, vidimus, Deus vicit.

Kara Mustafa Pasha was strangled with a silken bowstring on Christmas Day 1683 in Belgrade, on the sultan's orders, and his head sent to Istanbul — the standard Ottoman fate for grand viziers who had presided over major military disasters.

The Great Turkish War of 1683–1699 saw the Holy League retake Buda after one hundred and forty-five years of Ottoman rule, recover most of the Peloponnese for Venice, and push Russian forces toward the Crimean Tatars' territory, all while the Janissary corps mutinied repeatedly and refused to leave Istanbul.

The Treaty of Karlowitz in January 1699 — the first substantial Ottoman territorial losses to Christian powers since the empire's founding — ceded Hungary and Transylvania to the Habsburgs, Podolia to Poland-Lithuania, most of the Peloponnese to Venice, and Azov to Russia.

Karlowitz was also the first Ottoman treaty negotiated under English and Dutch mediation and the first acknowledging the Habsburg emperor as an equal sovereign rather than a tributary — the empire had entered the Westphalian diplomatic system as a subject rather than as a master.

The Tulip Era (1718–1730) under Ahmed III was the most cosmopolitan and leisure-oriented Ottoman court since Süleyman, featuring competitive tulip cultivation, nighttime garden viewing by candlelight on the backs of trained tortoises, and European-influenced Sa'dabad Palace architecture.

Ibrahim Müteferrika opened the first Ottoman press in Arabic-script Turkish in 1727, producing seventeen titles in twenty-three volumes over fifteen years — Ottoman printing had been delayed for two centuries while Sephardic, Greek, and Armenian presses had operated freely in the empire for the non-Muslim communities.

The Patrona Halil revolt of September 1730 — organized by an Albanian-born former bath attendant turned Janissary auxiliary — sacked Sa'dabad Palace, burned the tulip gardens, killed thirty senior officials, deposed Ahmed III, and ended the Tulip Era reforms within forty-eight hours.

title: Reform and Backlash
bullets: The Treaty of Küçük Kaynarca of 1774, ending the Russo-Turkish War of 1768–1774 in which the Russian fleet had sailed from the Baltic to destroy the Ottoman navy at Çeşme, granted Russia the right to act as protector of Ottoman Orthodox Christians — a provision invoked repeatedly over the next century to justify Russian intervention in Ottoman internal affairs.

Selim III's Nizam-ı Cedid reform program built a new infantry corps trained on European models by French drillmasters, funded from a separate treasury, and explicitly designed as an eventual replacement for the Janissaries — which was precisely why the Janissaries organized a revolt to destroy it.

Selim III was confined to the Kafes after the 1807 Janissary uprising cancelled his reforms, and was murdered there in July 1808 by order of Mustafa IV to prevent his restoration — one of only two Ottoman sultans killed by explicit sultanic command of a successor.

Mahmud II spent eighteen years from 1808 to 1826 carefully building his political base — consolidating control of the central administration, securing the Sheikh ul-Islam's religious endorsement, rebuilding the artillery corps — in patient preparation for a single decisive act against the Janissaries.

On June 14–15, 1826, the Janissary revolt began exactly as Mahmud had calculated, and his prepared artillery corps bombarded the barracks with grapeshot at point-blank range; the Auspicious Incident killed an estimated six thousand to twenty thousand Janissaries in Istanbul alone over four days.

The Auspicious Incident simultaneously destroyed the Janissary corps, suppressed the Bektashi Sufi order that had been its spiritual home, and eliminated in a single summer the four-hundred-and-sixty-three-year-old institution that had taken Constantinople and served every sultan since Murad I.

Greek independence (1821–1832), the first successful nationalist secession from Ottoman rule, was achieved only after a combined British-French-Russian fleet destroyed the Ottoman-Egyptian fleet at the Battle of Navarino in October 1827 — the Greeks had been largely defeated before European intervention reversed the outcome.

The Treaty of Hünkar İskelesi of 1833, signed after Mehmed Ali Pasha's armies from Egypt nearly reached Anatolia and were only stopped by Russian military intervention, granted Russia exclusive naval rights in the Bosporus and effectively made the Ottoman Empire a Russian protectorate for several years.

title: The Sick Man of Europe
bullets: The Tanzimat reforms launched in 1839 produced the empire's most sustained institutional transformation — importing French criminal and commercial codes, establishing secular nizamiye courts alongside the sharia courts, building a state primary and secondary school network, and replacing the autonomous ayan provincial system with centralized French departmental administration.

The Hatt-ı Hümayun edict of 1856, issued under explicit British and French pressure at the end of the Crimean War, formally granted non-Muslim subjects full legal equality in state law and opened state administrative careers to them — a formal dismantling of the millet-based legal hierarchy that had organized the empire for four centuries.

The Dolmabahçe Palace, completed in 1856 with fourteen tons of gold leaf in its ceilings and a four-and-a-half-ton crystal chandelier gifted by Queen Victoria, replaced Topkapı as the imperial residence and captured the Tanzimat era's aspiration to be recognized as part of European civilization — at enormous expense that contributed to the imperial debt crisis.

The Bulgarian Massacres of 1876 — conducted by bashi-bazouk irregulars and Circassian refugees, with an estimated twelve thousand to thirty thousand civilian deaths — were documented by American journalist Januarius MacGahan, and William Gladstone's pamphlet Bulgarian Horrors and the Question of the East fundamentally shifted British public opinion against Ottoman support.

The Russo-Turkish War of 1877–1878, followed by the Congress of Berlin under Bismarck's mediation, cost the empire approximately one-third of its European territory and roughly twenty percent of its total population within a single decade — Bulgaria, Romania, Serbia, Bosnia, and Cyprus were all effectively gone.

Abdülhamid II promulgated the first Ottoman Constitution in December 1876, allowed parliament to meet in March 1877, and suspended both in February 1878 citing the Russo-Turkish War emergency — a suspension that lasted thirty years.

The Hamidian Massacres of 1894–1896 killed an estimated one hundred thousand to three hundred thousand Armenian Christians across eastern Anatolia through organized operations by Hamidiye irregular cavalry and Ottoman regular troops — establishing the pattern that would be escalated catastrophically in 1915.

The Committee of Union and Progress, founded in 1889 by medical students at the Imperial Military Medical School and organized around CUP cells in the Balkan army garrisons, restored the 1876 Constitution in July 1908 within weeks of beginning its mutinies, without a single major battle.

Abdülhamid II's counter-coup attempt of April 1909 was suppressed by the CUP's Action Army marching from Salonika; he was deposed on April 27, confined to a villa, and died under house arrest in Istanbul in 1918 — the last major autocrat of the Ottoman dynasty.

title: The Great War and Aftermath
bullets: The First Balkan War of October 1912 to May 1913 overran Macedonia, Albania, Thrace, and most of the Aegean coast within weeks, with Salonika — the second-largest city in the empire and the CUP's own operational headquarters — falling to the Greeks on November 8, 1912.

By August 1913 the Ottoman state had lost approximately 83% of its European territory and 69% of its European population in two Balkan wars in under a year — the Balkan empire built by Murad I, Bayezid I, and Mehmed II between the 1360s and the 1450s was effectively gone.

The Three Pashas — Enver as War Minister, Talaat as Interior Minister, and Cemal as Naval Minister — entered the First World War on the side of Germany and Austria-Hungary in late October 1914, calculating that Germany's victory might recover lost Balkan and Caucasus territories; the bet cost the empire its existence.

Enver Pasha's winter offensive at the Battle of Sarıkamış in December 1914–January 1915 destroyed the Ottoman Third Army — perhaps 90,000 strong, caught without winter equipment in the eastern Anatolian highlands — losing perhaps three-quarters of its strength to combat, frostbite, and starvation.

The Gallipoli Campaign of April 1915 to January 1916 was the Ottoman war effort's one major strategic success, containing Allied landings under the divisional command of Mustafa Kemal Bey at age thirty-four, establishing his military reputation, and simultaneously creating the Anzac legend and destroying Winston Churchill's reputation as First Lord of the Admiralty.

The Armenian Genocide of 1915–1916, directed by Talaat Pasha as Interior Minister using Special Organization paramilitary units, systematic deportation marches through hostile terrain to Syrian desert concentration sites, and organized village massacres, killed an estimated one to one and a half million people out of a pre-war Armenian population of approximately two million.

Soghomon Tehlirian, an Armenian genocide survivor, assassinated Talaat Pasha in Berlin in March 1921 and was acquitted at trial on grounds of temporary insanity, with the genocide explicitly entered as evidence — the trial contributed to the postwar international legal vocabulary of crimes against humanity.

The Treaty of Sèvres of August 1920 would have reduced the Ottoman state to a rump territory in central Anatolia, ceding the Arab provinces to British and French mandates, western Anatolia and eastern Thrace to Greece, and eastern Anatolia to a planned Armenian state and Kurdish autonomous zone.

The Greco-Turkish War of 1919–1922, culminating in the Turkish nationalist offensive that broke the Greek army at Dumlupınar and retook Smyrna on September 9, 1922, destroyed the Sèvres settlement and established Mustafa Kemal as the de facto ruler of Turkey.

The last sultan Mehmed VI Vahdettin, having signed the catastrophic Sèvres treaty, fled Istanbul on a British warship on November 17, 1922, ending the thirty-six-sultan, six-hundred-and-twenty-three-year Ottoman dynastic succession that had begun with Osman I.

title: The Republic and What Endured
bullets: The Republic of Turkey was proclaimed on October 29, 1923, with Ankara — the central Anatolian city that had been the nationalist movement's wartime capital — replacing Istanbul as the new capital, deliberately breaking the five-century association between the dynasty and Constantinople.

The Caliphate, briefly separated from the sultanate under Abdülmecid II, was abolished in March 1924, ending the eight-hundred-year institutional continuity that had passed from the Abbasids to the Mamluks to the Ottomans — it has not been reconstituted since.

The 1926 adoption of the Swiss Civil Code abolished polygamy, introduced equal civil rights for women (including the vote in 1934, earlier than in many European states), and replaced Ottoman sharia-based personal status law with secular state marriage as the only legally recognized form.

The Latin alphabet, adopted in 1928 in roughly six months from decision to legal mandate, severed modern Turks from the six centuries of Ottoman-script writing so thoroughly that within a generation most could not read documents written before the change without specialized scholarly training.

The Turkish Language Society's systematic 1930s purge of Persian and Arabic loan-words from Ottoman Turkish — which had constituted perhaps half of formal vocabulary — produced a linguistic rupture roughly equivalent to the difference between modern English and Anglo-Saxon Old English.

The Treaty of Lausanne of July 1923 formalized a population exchange that deported approximately 1.2 million Greeks from Anatolia to Greece and approximately 400,000 Turks from Greece to Turkey, conducted on the basis of religion rather than language or self-identification — the largest state-organized population transfer before the partition of British India in 1947.

The Sunni Hanafi Islamic tradition, the mosques, the five-times-daily call to prayer, the Ottoman cuisine (kebabs, börek, baklava, mezes, the elaborate palace pastry traditions), the Ottoman classical music, and the Sinan-era mosque skyline of Istanbul all survived the Republican institutional rupture essentially intact.

The borders of modern Iraq, Syria, Jordan, Israel, Lebanon, and Saudi Arabia were drawn after 1918 by Allied mandate authorities, sometimes following and sometimes overriding Ottoman administrative lines — and the various twentieth- and twenty-first-century conflicts of the Middle East involve disputes over populations and arrangements that trace in some part to the Ottoman dissolution.

The Sunni-Shi'a divide institutionalized at the Battle of Chaldiran in 1514 — with the Ottomans as the Sunni superpower and the Safavids as the Shi'a counter-state — continues to operate in modern form in the tensions between Saudi Arabia and Iran, in Iraqi and Lebanese sectarian politics, and in the religious geography of the contemporary Middle East.
